common_name: Hidcote English Lavender (Compact Deep-Purple English Lavender)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; 'Hidcote' does not come true from seed and must be propagated by cuttings or purchased as a named cultivar; it is compact and well-suited to formal plantings, edging, container growing, and small gardens where standard English lavender varieties grow too large.

directions: Plant in full sun with excellent drainage (amended sandy or gravelly soil if needed) and avoid heavy clay or consistently moist soils; trim after flowering to maintain compact shape; in zones 8–9 plant in fall for establishment before summer heat; Hidcote lavender requires excellent air circulation to prevent fungal issues in humid climates. Hidcote is one of the most beloved and widely planted English lavender cultivars in the world, named for the famous English Arts and Crafts garden Hidcote Manor in Gloucestershire — a National Trust property renowned for its rare plants and structured garden design, where this compact, very dark-purple lavender was popularized. The cultivar has been a standard in English lavender since the early 20th century and is recognized by its distinctive characteristics: very compact, tightly mounding habit (12–18 inches rather than the 24–36 of open-pollinated English lavenders), intensely deep purple-violet flowers (darker than almost all other English lavender varieties), and silver-gray foliage. The deep color makes Hidcote one of the most visually striking lavenders for formal planting designs, knot gardens, edging borders, and formal herb gardens. The fragrance is intensely sweet in the characteristic English lavender style — considered by many perfumers to be the most refined and complex of all lavender types, with a floral-camphorous-sweet balance that is the reference fragrance for culinary lavender and lavender products. Hidcote is the lavender for people who want compact, tidy, deeply colored plants for formal or small-space settings.

common_name: Grosso Lavandin (Commercial Fragrance Lavender, Provence Lavender)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in full sun with excellent drainage; Grosso is a lavandin hybrid (L. angustifolia × L. latifolia) that is sterile and must be propagated by cuttings; it grows larger and faster than English lavender with much longer flower spikes that are the dominant type grown for the commercial lavender oil industry in Provence, France.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained to dry soil; Grosso grows larger than English lavenders and needs 24–36 inches of space; shear spent flower stalks after bloom to prevent woody sprawling; in zones 8–10 where summer humidity is high, Grosso performs better than English lavender due to the L. latifolia parentage contributing some heat tolerance. Grosso is the world's dominant commercial lavender variety — it covers the rolling purple fields of the Provence and Drôme regions of France that define the iconic 'lavender landscape' image of southern France, and it is the primary source of lavender oil in the global fragrance and cosmetics industry. As a lavandin hybrid (a sterile cross between English lavender, L. angustifolia, and spike lavender, L. latifolia), Grosso inherits characteristics of both parents: the floral-sweet fragrance of English lavender combined with the higher camphor content, larger plant size, and greater heat tolerance of spike lavender. This combination makes the Grosso essential oil slightly different from pure English lavender oil — with a more complex, slightly camphor-accented fragrance that is valuable in perfumery but considered less pure for culinary use. For garden use, Grosso produces the longest, most striking flower spikes of any commonly grown lavender — up to 12–16 inches — in a dense, voluminous display that is far more dramatic than any English lavender. Harvested just before fully open for maximum essential oil content; used in sachets, potpourri, culinary (in small amounts), and craft arrangements.

common_name: Phenomenal Lavender (Heat-Humidity Tolerant Lavandin for Southern Gardens)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring or fall in full sun with excellent drainage; Phenomenal is a lavandin hybrid selected for superior performance in hot, humid climates where standard English lavenders rot or fail — it is the most widely recommended lavender for the Southeastern US, Texas, and other regions with hot, humid summers that are particularly hard on lavender; significantly more disease-resistant than any English lavender variety.

directions: Plant in full sun with excellent drainage; in humid climates amend clay soil with coarse sand or gravel; excellent air circulation is essential; in zones 7–9 with humid summers, plant in fall for establishment; Phenomenal resists the root rots and fungal diseases that kill standard lavenders in humid heat, making it essentially the only lavender that can be grown reliably as a long-lived perennial in the humid South. Phenomenal is a lavandin hybrid (Lavandula × intermedia) that was selected specifically for exceptional performance in hot, humid climates — a characteristic set that very few lavender varieties possess, since most lavenders originate from the Mediterranean region and are adapted to hot, dry summers rather than the humid heat of the American Southeast. The selection and introduction of Phenomenal represented a significant advance for Southern gardeners who had long struggled to grow lavender as a lasting perennial in zones 7–9 with high summer humidity — the conditions that cause root rot and crown fungal diseases in English lavenders within 1–3 years. Phenomenal's superior disease resistance in humid conditions comes from a combination of its thicker, more robust stems, greater inherent resistance to Botrytis and Phytophthora, and possibly greater ability to shed moisture from the crown. The plants are large, vigorous, and productive, with long silver-gray spikes and a strongly fragrant, slightly camphor-accented scent. For zone 9a Texas and Gulf Coast gardens, Phenomenal is the practical choice when other lavenders have repeatedly failed.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container transplants in spring or fall in full sun, well-drained soil; Arp is the most cold-hardy commonly available rosemary cultivar, surviving temperatures to 0°F or below in protected locations — making it a viable perennial in zones 6–7 where standard rosemary frequently winter-kills; it was discovered in Arp, Texas, and introduced by Madalene Hill, the Texas herb expert, in the 1970s.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; rosemary prefers alkaline to neutral soil (pH 6.5–8.0) and tolerates poor, rocky, sandy soils far better than rich garden soil which promotes lush but frost-tender growth; prune lightly after flowering to maintain shape; Arp's cold hardiness makes it worth trying in zone 6 with winter protection in sheltered spots. Arp rosemary is one of the most important named rosemary cultivars for American gardens, selected for its exceptional cold hardiness compared to standard rosemary (Salvia rosmarinus, formerly Rosmarinus officinalis), which is reliably perennial only to zone 7 or 8 depending on drainage and shelter. The variety was discovered by Madalene Hill, the pioneering Texas herb expert and founder of the Festival Hill herb garden in Round Top, Texas, growing in the East Texas town of Arp — a small town in Smith County where a rosemary plant had survived an unusually cold winter that killed most other rosemaries in the area. Hill propagated the surviving plant and introduced it to the herb gardening world, where its superior cold hardiness was documented and confirmed in trials at locations as far north as zone 5 in sheltered exposures. The cold hardiness of Arp is thought to come from a slightly different chemical composition of its essential oils that provides better antifreeze protection at cellular level. The culinary quality and fragrance are equivalent to any standard rosemary variety — piney, resinous, intensely aromatic — and the upright habit and light blue flowers are identical in garden appearance to standard rosemary. A significant contribution to herb garden culture, introduced by one of the most important figures in the American herb movement.
sources: Festival Hill — Madalene Hill Herb Garden

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container transplants in spring or fall in full sun with excellent drainage; Salem is a semi-upright to cascading cold-hardy rosemary cultivar considered nearly as cold-hardy as Arp (surviving to -5°F in some reports) with particularly good culinary flavor and a graceful, slightly arching habit that makes it attractive as both an herb plant and an ornamental garden shrub.

directions: Plant in full sun with excellent drainage; rosemary is particularly sensitive to wet winter soils which cause crown rot; Salem's semi-cascading habit makes it attractive planted at the edge of raised beds, retaining walls, or terraces where the arching stems can spill gracefully downward; harvest sprigs as needed throughout the growing season. Salem is a cold-hardy named rosemary cultivar that combines the primary traits that herb gardeners seek in a rosemary plant: excellent cold hardiness comparable to Arp, superior culinary flavor and fragrance, and attractive garden presence with a graceful semi-upright to slightly arching habit that is more ornamental than the sometimes rigid upright form of standard culinary rosemary. The variety is considered by many herb specialists to have slightly better culinary quality than Arp — the fragrance and flavor is more intensely resinous and sweet-piney with less medicinal sharpness. Salem is named for Salem, Oregon (not Salem, Massachusetts), reflecting its introduction and testing in the Pacific Northwest where it demonstrated exceptional cold hardiness. In zone 9a and the Gulf South, Salem rosemary is one of the most reliable long-lived perennial rosemary cultivars alongside Arp and Tuscan Blue — the combination of good cold hardiness and heat tolerance makes it adaptable across a very wide geographic range. The blue to pale blue-violet flowers in winter and spring are an important early nectar source for bees before most other garden plants have begun to bloom.
